title: Cat Aggressively Attacking Own Tail: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat attacking its tail? Learn about Feline Hyperesthesia, potential injuries, and when to seek veterinary care in this triage guide.
content_html:
What is it?

When a cat aggressively attacks its own tail, it is often more than just a case of the "zoomies." This behavior frequently indicates a medical or neurological condition such as Feline Hyperesthesia Syndrome (FHS), nerve damage, or localized pain from an injury or infection. It can also be a manifestation of extreme stress or a compulsive disorder where the cat no longer recognizes its tail as part of its own body.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for tail-chasing and self-aggression is Medium. While usually not an immediate life-threatening crisis, it is a significant welfare concern because the cat is often in distress or pain. You should contact a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. However, if the cat has caused significant self-trauma, such as deep bleeding wounds or a visible tail deformity, seek veterinary attention more urgently.

Why a photo helps triage

Because these episodes can be intermittent, your cat may act perfectly normal during a physical exam. Taking a video of the behavior or high-quality photos of any skin irritation, hair loss, or wounds on the tail provides your vet with crucial diagnostic evidence. These visuals help determine if the issue is dermatological, neurological, or behavioral in nature.

faqs.question: What is Feline Hyperesthesia Syndrome?
faqs.answer: It is a condition that causes a cat's skin to be hypersensitive, often leading to skin rippling, frantic grooming, and tail attacking.

faqs.question: Can fleas cause a cat to attack its tail?
faqs.answer: Yes, flea allergy dermatitis often affects the base of the tail and can cause intense itching and agitation.

faqs.question: How can I stop my cat from hurting themselves?
faqs.answer: Using a soft recovery collar (e-collar) can provide temporary protection while you wait for a veterinary appointment.
